planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average soil in full sun to partial shade after last frost; barbados cherry is drought-tolerant once established but fruits most heavily with regular irrigation during the three to four annual fruiting cycles; protect from frost in zone 9a.

directions: Root semi-hardwood cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average soil in full sun; barbados cherry (acerola, West Indian cherry) is a subtropical shrub or small tree that produces cherry-sized, three-lobed, bright red to orange-red fruits containing the highest natural concentration of Vitamin C of any food crop — approximately 65 times the Vitamin C of orange juice by weight. In south Florida, Texas Gulf Coast, and zone 9–11 climates, established plants produce three to four fruiting cycles annually, yielding heavy crops of sweet-tart, highly perishable fruits that must be processed immediately (fresh eating, juice, smoothies, frozen puree) as they deteriorate within hours of harvest. The plant is an attractive, dense, glossy-leaved evergreen shrub with small, pink, orchid-like flowers and is often grown as a fruiting hedge or informal screen. Productive and relatively pest-free in tropical and subtropical climates. 'B-17', 'Manoa Sweet', and 'Florida Sweet' are improved selections with larger, sweeter fruit.

planting.note: Direct-sow seeds (nick or scarify seed coat for improved germination) or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, average to poor, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun in spring; senna establishes rapidly from seed and may bloom the first year from seed; it dies to the ground in zone 8 but rebounds vigorously from the root.

directions: Direct-sow scarified seeds or plant container specimens in well-drained, average to poor soil in full sun; flowery senna (Argentine senna) is a fast-growing South American shrub that provides one of the most spectacular late-summer and fall flower displays available in the Gulf Coast garden — smothered from late August through November in clusters of brilliant, clear-yellow, five-petaled flowers that glow with exceptional intensity as other summer plants fatigue. It is critically important as the larval host plant for the Cloudless Sulphur butterfly (Phoebis sennae) and the Sleepy Orange butterfly (Abaeis nicippe) — expect to find caterpillars feeding on the foliage throughout summer, which is a sign of success rather than a problem. The plant is fast-growing, drought-tolerant, and thrives in poor, dry soil — conditions that suit it for problem, hot, dry corners of the garden. In zone 8 it dies to the ground in winter but rebounds from the root to 6–8 feet by midsummer. Excellent screening plant for late-season butterfly gardens.

planting.note: Divide basal suckers or plant container-grown specimens in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile soil in partial shade to full sun in spring after frost; pagoda flower spreads by underground stolons and requires ample space — allow 6 feet on each side for a mature, freely suckering clump.

directions: Divide suckers or plant container specimens in moist, well-drained, average to fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; pagoda flower (scarlet clerodendrum, tower of gold) is a Southeast Asian tropical shrub that produces one of the most impressive flower structures of any tropical plant — enormous, upright, tiered panicles reaching 18–24 inches in height, composed of hundreds of small, intensely orange-scarlet, tubular flowers arranged in dense whorls that give the flower head the appearance of a brilliantly colored pagoda structure. The large, dark-green, boldly lobed leaves are themselves dramatic, and the entire plant creates a powerful tropical statement in summer and fall borders. It spreads steadily by underground suckers to form colonies, which can be controlled by mowing the perimeter. Hummingbirds and butterflies are strongly attracted to the flower heads. Semi-shade tolerant — performs well in dappled light under high canopy trees. In zone 9 top growth is sometimes killed by frost but resprouts vigorously from the root. 'Starburst' is a popular selection.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Root stem-tip cuttings in moist perlite or plant container-grown specimens in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile soil in partial shade after frost; caricature plant is excellent in partial shade where other tropical foliage plants such as coleus and impatiens also perform well.

directions: Root stem-tip cuttings or plant container specimens in moist, well-drained, average to fertile soil in partial shade; caricature plant is a tropical shrub from New Guinea and the Pacific Islands that is grown entirely for its extraordinary variegated foliage — large, shiny, oval leaves boldly streaked, blotched, and suffused with yellow, cream, rose-pink, or purple-red markings on a dark-green background, with the irregular patterning suggesting (to imaginative observers) the exaggerated features of cartoon faces or caricatures, giving the plant its common name. The color intensity and pattern of the leaf markings vary with the amount of light — plants in brighter partial shade develop the most vivid contrasts, while plants in deeper shade become more uniformly green. Small, tubular, purplish-red flowers appear occasionally on mature plants in tropical conditions but are secondary to the foliage display. Widely grown as a houseplant and greenhouse specimen in cool climates; in zone 10–12 it functions as a year-round outdoor foliage shrub of considerable ornamental impact.

planting.note: Root stem-tip cuttings in warm, moist perlite or plant container-grown specimens in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile soil in partial shade after frost; pseuderanthemum colors most intensely in bright indirect light — deep shade causes the purple coloring to fade toward green.

directions: Root stem-tip cuttings or plant container specimens in moist, well-drained, average to fertile soil in partial shade; purple false eranthemum (Jacob's coat, eldorado) is a Pacific Islands tropical shrub grown for its spectacular, large, glossy, dark-purple to maroon leaves dramatically overlaid with scattered spots, streaks, and blotches of pink, rose, gold, and white — one of the most richly colored foliage plants available for tropical and subtropical gardens. The unusual combination of dark background coloration with bright-colored irregular markings creates a bold, jewel-like quality unlike any other landscape shrub. Small, pale-lavender to white, tubular flowers appear in terminal spikes throughout the year in frost-free climates but are secondary to the remarkable foliage. Excellent combined with chartreuse-leaved plants (golden pothos, lime coleus) for high-contrast tropical compositions. Grown as a houseplant in cool climates; in zone 10–12 it forms an outdoor foliage shrub of 2–4 feet. 'Tricolor' and 'Rubrum' are cultivar selections emphasizing different color combinations.
